politician.name: Barack Hussein Obama II
politician.professional_background: Constitutional law professor; U.S. Senator (IL); community organizer.
politician.profile_narrative: Obama's two-term presidency (2009-2017) reshaped U.S. healthcare with the Affordable Care Act and finished with a record across climate, immigration, criminal justice, and foreign policy that ran into a Republican-controlled Congress for most of the second term. This profile grades his 2008 and 2012 campaign promises against eight years in office.

promises.promise_text: Reform health insurance: cover the uninsured, end pre-existing-condition denials, allow children to stay on parents' plan to age 26.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act (Pub.L. 111-148) was signed March 23, 2010. The pre-existing-condition prohibition and age-26 dependent coverage rule are codified in the law and remained in force throughout his presidency.

promises.promise_text: End the Iraq War and bring combat troops home.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Combat operations formally ended August 2010; remaining U.S. troops withdrew by December 2011 per the Bush-era Status of Forces Agreement. However, U.S. forces returned to Iraq beginning June 2014 to counter ISIS, with roughly 5,000 troops in-country by end-of-term.

promises.promise_text: Close the Guantánamo Bay detention facility.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Executive Order 13492 (Jan 22, 2009) ordered closure within one year. Congress blocked transfer of detainees to U.S. soil via the FY2011 NDAA and subsequent restrictions. Guantánamo remained open at end-of-term with 41 detainees.

promises.promise_text: Pass comprehensive immigration reform with a path to citizenship.
promises.verdict_reasoning: S.744 passed the Senate 68-32 in June 2013 but House Speaker Boehner refused to bring it to a floor vote. The DACA executive action (June 2012) and DAPA (Nov 2014, enjoined by courts) did not constitute statutory comprehensive reform.

promises.promise_text: Enact a cap-and-trade system to address climate change.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The American Clean Energy and Security Act (Waxman-Markey, H.R.2454) passed the House 219-212 in June 2009. The Senate companion never reached a floor vote and was abandoned in July 2010. No subsequent cap-and-trade bill advanced.

promises.promise_text: Rejoin or strengthen international climate agreements.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The United States signed the Paris Agreement on Apr 22, 2016 and formally accepted it Sep 3, 2016 alongside China. The U.S.-China Joint Announcement (Nov 2014) set the precedent that enabled the Paris terms.

promises.promise_text: Stand firm against further tax cuts for top earners.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The American Taxpayer Relief Act of 2012 (Jan 2, 2013) raised the top marginal rate from 35% to 39.6% on incomes above $400k/$450k, but extended the Bush rates permanently for incomes below that. Some Bush-era cuts on top earners were thus extended for years before the partial rollback.

promises.promise_text: Eliminate Osama bin Laden and disrupt al-Qaeda leadership.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Operation Neptune Spear killed Osama bin Laden in Abbottabad, Pakistan on May 2, 2011. Subsequent drone-strike campaigns through 2016 killed senior al-Qaeda operational leaders including Anwar al-Awlaki (Sep 2011) and Atiyah Abd al-Rahman (Aug 2011).

promises.promise_text: Repeal Don't Ask, Don't Tell.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The Don't Ask, Don't Tell Repeal Act of 2010 (Pub.L. 111-321) was signed Dec 22, 2010. Implementation completed Sep 20, 2011 after the certification requirements were satisfied.

promises.promise_text: Negotiate a nuclear deal with Iran.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action was concluded July 14, 2015 between Iran and the P5+1. UN Security Council Resolution 2231 endorsed it July 20, 2015. The agreement remained in force at the end of his term.

promises.promise_text: Reduce student loan burden via income-based repayment.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Income-Based Repayment was created by the College Cost Reduction and Access Act of 2007 but expanded substantially via Pay As You Earn (Dec 2012) and Revised PAYE (Dec 2015), capping payments at 10% of discretionary income with forgiveness after 20-25 years.

promises.promise_text: Pass meaningful gun-violence-prevention legislation after Sandy Hook.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The Manchin-Toomey background-check amendment to S.649 fell six votes short of cloture (54-46) on April 17, 2013. No major federal gun legislation passed in the remaining four years of the term despite repeated executive-branch attempts.

politician.name: Joseph Robinette Biden Jr. (Vice Presidency)
politician.profile_narrative: Biden's vice presidency (2009-2017) under Obama. Promises here are limited to the small set of commitments Biden personally made during the 2008 and 2012 campaigns about his portfolio (Iraq drawdown, Recovery Act oversight, Cancer Moonshot, gun-violence task force). A separate profile will eventually cover his presidency (2021-2025).

promises.promise_text: Oversee the Recovery Act rollout transparently.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Biden chaired the Recovery Act implementation team. Recovery.gov launched Feb 17, 2009 with publicly searchable contract and grant data; GAO and Recovery Accountability and Transparency Board reports documented oversight activity.

promises.promise_text: Lead the gun-violence task force after Sandy Hook.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Biden led the task force and delivered 23 executive actions on Jan 16, 2013 (background-check enhancements, mental-health reporting). The signature legislative goal — Manchin-Toomey background checks — failed cloture 54-46 on April 17, 2013.

promises.promise_text: Lead the Cancer Moonshot initiative.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The 21st Century Cures Act (Pub.L. 114-255), signed Dec 13, 2016, included $1.8 billion in Cancer Moonshot funding over seven years. Biden delivered the Moonshot Task Force report Oct 17, 2016.

promises.promise_text: Support responsible withdrawal from Iraq.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Combat operations ended Aug 2010; remaining forces departed Dec 2011 per the Status of Forces Agreement. U.S. troops re-deployed to Iraq June 2014 to counter ISIS, complicating the 'responsible' framing.

promises.promise_text: Promote middle-class economic priorities through Middle Class Task Force.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Biden chaired the task force, which issued reports on retirement security, college access, and child care. Concrete policy wins were limited — Saver's Credit expansion proposed but not enacted, and many task force recommendations stalled in Congress.

promises.promise_text: Help broker the Bipartisan Budget Act compromises.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Biden was the lead negotiator with McConnell on the 2010 tax-cut extension deal, the 2011 debt-ceiling resolution, and the American Taxpayer Relief Act of 2012 (passed Jan 1, 2013), all of which averted near-immediate fiscal crises.

promises.promise_text: Reinforce U.S. commitments to NATO and European allies.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Biden made nine official trips to Europe as VP, led the U.S. response to Russia's 2014 annexation of Crimea (Visa/MasterCard suspensions, Section 311 financial sanctions), and articulated the Article 5 commitment in Munich Security Conference addresses.

promises.promise_text: Oversee Violence Against Women Act renewals.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Biden, original 1994 VAWA author, advocated for the 2013 reauthorization (Pub.L. 113-4, signed March 7, 2013), which extended protections to LGBT victims, Native American women on tribal lands, and undocumented immigrants.

promises.promise_text: Lead U.S. policy toward Latin America.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Biden made multiple trips to Mexico, Colombia, Central America. The Alliance for Prosperity (2014) directed $750M to address Northern Triangle migration root causes. Migration from the region nonetheless surged through 2016.

promises.promise_text: Support marriage-equality recognition.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Biden publicly endorsed same-sex marriage on Meet the Press, May 6, 2012 — three days before Obama did the same. The administration filed an amicus brief in U.S. v. Windsor (2013) and Obergefell v. Hodges (2015), the latter establishing nationwide recognition.

politician.name: Paul Davis Ryan Jr.
politician.professional_background: U.S. Representative (WI-1) 1999-2019; House Budget Committee chair; Ways and Means chair; 54th Speaker of the House (2015-2019); 2012 Republican vice-presidential nominee.
politician.profile_narrative: Ryan served 20 years in the House, ascending to Speaker (Oct 2015 to Jan 2019). This profile grades his 2016-cycle Speaker tenure (Jan 2017-Jan 2019), the most consequential window covering the 115th Congress under unified Republican control. He chose not to seek re-election in 2018.

promises.promise_text: Repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The American Health Care Act passed the House 217-213 on May 4, 2017 with Ryan's support, but failed in the Senate. The 'skinny repeal' amendment (H.R.1628) failed 49-51 on July 28, 2017 (McCain, Collins, Murkowski no). The ACA remained in force at end-of-term.

promises.promise_text: Pass comprehensive tax reform.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (Pub.L. 115-97) passed the House 224-201 on Dec 19, 2017 and was signed Dec 22, 2017. It cut the corporate rate from 35% to 21%, lowered individual rates through 2025, and doubled the standard deduction.

promises.promise_text: Confirm conservative judges, especially to the Supreme Court.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Justice Neil Gorsuch was confirmed April 7, 2017 (54-45). Justice Brett Kavanaugh was confirmed Oct 6, 2018 (50-48). 85 Article III judges were confirmed during Ryan's speakership, including 30 circuit-court judges.

promises.promise_text: Reform entitlements (Medicare and Social Security).
promises.verdict_reasoning: Ryan's signature 'Path to Prosperity' Medicare-premium-support proposal did not receive a House floor vote during the 115th Congress. Social Security was not addressed in legislation. Ryan acknowledged this regret in his Apr 2018 retirement announcement.

promises.promise_text: Pass border-security funding and enforcement reforms.
promises.verdict_reasoning: H.R.4760 (Securing America's Future Act) failed the House 193-231 on June 21, 2018. A compromise bill that paired DACA relief with $25B in wall funding also failed. The FY2018 omnibus did include $1.6B in border-fencing funds but well below the $25B requested.

promises.promise_text: Cut regulations to spur economic growth.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Congress used the Congressional Review Act 16 times in early 2017 to overturn Obama-era rules (vs. just one prior CRA use in U.S. history). The 115th Congress passed and Trump signed all 16 resolutions, a substantial dereg push by historical standards.

promises.promise_text: Pass criminal-justice reform.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The First Step Act (Pub.L. 115-391) passed the House 358-36 on Dec 20, 2018 with Ryan's vocal support and was signed Dec 21, 2018. It reduced mandatory minimums, expanded compassionate release, and incentivized recidivism-reduction programs.

promises.promise_text: Increase military spending.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The Bipartisan Budget Act of 2018 raised defense caps by $80B for FY2018 and $85B for FY2019. The John S. McCain NDAA for FY2019 (Pub.L. 115-232) authorized $717B in defense spending, an $82B increase over FY2017.

promises.promise_text: Address opioid epidemic.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The Comprehensive Addiction and Recovery Act (Pub.L. 114-198, July 2016) and the SUPPORT for Patients and Communities Act (Pub.L. 115-271, Oct 24, 2018) were both signed during Ryan's speakership. The latter directed billions in funding and expanded Medicaid coverage for SUD treatment.

promises.promise_text: Investigate IRS-targeting and other oversight commitments.
promises.verdict_reasoning: House Ways and Means under Ryan referred IRS Commissioner John Koskinen for impeachment May 2016; impeachment effort died in House Judiciary. The IRS issued a public apology and paid $3.5M to settle Tea Party group lawsuits in 2017.

politician.name: Orrin Grant Hatch
politician.professional_background: U.S. Senator (UT) 1977-2019; longest-serving Republican senator in history; Senate Finance Committee chair; Senate Pro Tempore (2015-2019).
politician.profile_narrative: Hatch served 42 years in the Senate, retiring in 2019 at the end of his seventh term. This profile grades his final 2016-cycle term (Jan 2013-Jan 2019), focusing on his Finance Committee chair role and the tax reform he co-authored.

promises.promise_text: Pass comprehensive tax reform.
promises.verdict_reasoning: As Finance Committee chair, Hatch was the Senate co-author of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (Pub.L. 115-97), which passed the Senate 51-48 on Dec 20, 2017 and was signed Dec 22, 2017.

promises.promise_text: Repeal the Affordable Care Act.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Hatch voted yes on the 'skinny repeal' amendment (Health Care Freedom Act) which failed 49-51 on July 28, 2017. The ACA remained in force at end-of-term, though the TCJA zeroed out the individual mandate penalty.

promises.promise_text: Reauthorize CHIP (Children's Health Insurance Program).
promises.verdict_reasoning: Hatch co-authored the long-term CHIP reauthorization included in the Bipartisan Budget Act of 2018 (Pub.L. 115-123, Feb 9, 2018), extending CHIP funding for 10 years — the longest extension in CHIP history.

promises.promise_text: Confirm conservative federal judges.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Hatch voted yes on Gorsuch (April 7, 2017, 54-45) and Kavanaugh (Oct 6, 2018, 50-48). He supported the elimination of the filibuster for Supreme Court nominations in April 2017.

promises.promise_text: Promote Utah public-lands priorities.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Hatch championed the proclamation reducing Bears Ears National Monument by 85% and Grand Staircase-Escalante by 47% (Dec 4, 2017 proclamations), a long-standing Utah Republican priority.

promises.promise_text: Address pharmaceutical drug pricing.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Hatch, a long-time recipient of pharmaceutical industry donations, opposed Medicare drug-price negotiation throughout this term and successfully kept it out of the TCJA. No major statutory drug-pricing reform passed during the 115th Congress.

promises.promise_text: Pass criminal-justice sentencing reform.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Hatch was a key Senate Republican supporter of the First Step Act (Pub.L. 115-391), which passed the Senate 87-12 on Dec 18, 2018 and was signed Dec 21, 2018.

promises.promise_text: Hold Russia accountable for 2016 election interference.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Hatch voted yes on the Countering America's Adversaries Through Sanctions Act (Pub.L. 115-44, signed Aug 2, 2017, 98-2 Senate vote). He did not, however, publicly criticize Trump's softer Russia framings.

promises.promise_text: Sustain U.S. commitment to free trade.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Hatch publicly criticized Trump's Section 232 steel and aluminum tariffs (March 2018) and the trade war with China, but did not move legislation to revoke the tariff authority. USMCA was renegotiated rather than NAFTA preserved.

promises.promise_text: Strengthen the Senate institution and bipartisan norms.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Hatch supported nuking the filibuster for Supreme Court nominations (April 2017), a major escalation of partisan procedure. He also voted yes on the nuclear option for executive-branch nominations procedurally enforced through this term.

politician.name: Jeffry Lane Flake
politician.current_status: Former senator (2019); U.S. Ambassador to Turkey (2022-2024)
politician.professional_background: U.S. Senator (AZ) 2013-2019; previously U.S. Representative (AZ-6) 2001-2013; chose not to seek re-election in 2018.
politician.profile_narrative: Flake served one Senate term (2013-2019). He chose not to seek re-election in October 2017, citing 'the present coarseness of our national politics.' This profile grades his single Senate term — one of the more clearly closed records in the directory.

promises.promise_text: Pass comprehensive immigration reform.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Flake co-authored the Gang of Eight S.744 bill, which passed the Senate 68-32 on June 27, 2013. Speaker Boehner declined to bring it to a House floor vote and it died in the 113th Congress. No subsequent comprehensive bill advanced during Flake's term.

promises.promise_text: Reduce earmarks and federal spending discipline.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Flake maintained his long-standing anti-earmark record. He released annual 'Wastebook' reports cataloging spending he opposed and voted no on multiple omnibus packages including the Bipartisan Budget Act of 2018.

promises.promise_text: Vote to confirm well-qualified conservative judges.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Flake voted yes on Gorsuch (April 7, 2017) and Kavanaugh (Oct 6, 2018, after his famous 'further FBI investigation' demand the prior week). He voted to confirm dozens of Trump circuit-court nominees.

promises.promise_text: Restore congressional oversight of trade authority.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Flake introduced multiple resolutions to disapprove Trump's Section 232 tariffs, but none reached a floor vote during his term. The Bicameral Congressional Trade Authority Act (2018) did not advance.

promises.promise_text: Repeal the Affordable Care Act.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Flake voted yes on the 'skinny repeal' amendment (failed 49-51, July 28, 2017). The ACA remained in force at end-of-term despite the TCJA zeroing the mandate penalty.

promises.promise_text: Pass meaningful tax reform.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Flake voted yes on the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (Pub.L. 115-97), which passed the Senate 51-48 on Dec 20, 2017.

promises.promise_text: Preserve federal lands and parks.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Flake supported the John D. Dingell Conservation, Management, and Recreation Act (Pub.L. 116-9), which was negotiated during the 115th Congress and signed March 12, 2019. He did not publicly oppose the Bears Ears/Grand Staircase reductions of Dec 2017.

promises.promise_text: Restore civil discourse and traditional Senate norms.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Flake delivered the high-profile 'present coarseness' floor speech announcing retirement (Oct 24, 2017). He occasionally crossed Trump publicly but supported most Trump nominees and the elimination of the SCOTUS filibuster (April 2017).

promises.promise_text: Address the federal deficit and debt.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The federal deficit rose from $585B in FY2016 to $984B in FY2019. The TCJA Flake supported is estimated by CBO to have added $1.9T to debt over a decade. He voted no on the FY2018 omnibus but the trajectory of debt accelerated through his term.

promises.promise_text: Defend the rule of law and Special Counsel Mueller investigation.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Flake co-sponsored the Special Counsel Independence and Integrity Act (S.2644), forcing Senate Judiciary Committee passage 14-7 on April 26, 2018. He publicly threatened to block judicial nominations until McConnell allowed a floor vote on the bill, though the floor vote never occurred.

politician.name: Robert Phillips Corker Jr.
politician.professional_background: U.S. Senator (TN) 2007-2019; Senate Foreign Relations Committee chair (2015-2019); previously mayor of Chattanooga (2001-2005).
politician.profile_narrative: Corker served two Senate terms, retiring in 2019. This profile grades his 2012-cycle second term (Jan 2013-Jan 2019), focusing on his role as Foreign Relations chair during the early Trump era.

promises.promise_text: Ensure congressional review of any Iran nuclear deal.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Corker authored the Iran Nuclear Agreement Review Act of 2015 (Pub.L. 114-17), signed May 22, 2015. The bill passed the Senate 98-1 and gave Congress 60 days to review the JCPOA.

promises.promise_text: Repeal the Affordable Care Act.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Corker voted yes on the skinny repeal (failed 49-51, July 28, 2017). The ACA remained in force.

promises.promise_text: Reform GSEs (Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac).
promises.verdict_reasoning: Corker co-authored the Johnson-Crapo bill (S.1217) in 2014 to wind down Fannie and Freddie. It passed the Senate Banking Committee 13-9 but never reached a floor vote. No subsequent GSE-reform bill advanced during his term.

promises.promise_text: Confirm well-qualified federal judges.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Corker voted yes on Gorsuch (April 7, 2017) and Kavanaugh (Oct 6, 2018), plus the 85 lower-court judges confirmed during the 115th Congress.

promises.promise_text: Pass tax reform.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Corker initially voted no on TCJA in committee citing deficit concerns, then voted yes on final passage (Dec 20, 2017, 51-48) after a provision benefiting real-estate LLCs was added — a vote he received public criticism for.

promises.promise_text: Address the federal deficit.
promises.verdict_reasoning: The federal deficit nearly doubled from $585B (FY2016) to $984B (FY2019). The TCJA he ultimately supported added an estimated $1.9T to debt over 10 years per CBO.

promises.promise_text: Restore congressional War Powers authority.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Corker introduced a new AUMF in April 2018 with Sen. Kaine but it did not reach a floor vote. He did vote yes on S.J.Res.54 to end U.S. support for the Saudi-led coalition in Yemen, which passed the Senate 56-41 on Dec 13, 2018.

promises.promise_text: Hold Russia accountable for 2016 election interference.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Corker voted yes on the Countering America's Adversaries Through Sanctions Act (Pub.L. 115-44, Aug 2, 2017, 98-2). He publicly criticized Trump's softer Russia framings, including in his Oct 2017 'adult day care' tweet.

promises.promise_text: Reauthorize NDAA and support military readiness.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Corker voted yes on FY2018 NDAA (Pub.L. 115-91), FY2019 John S. McCain NDAA (Pub.L. 115-232, $717B authorization), and the Bipartisan Budget Act of 2018 raising defense caps.

promises.promise_text: Support free trade and oppose unilateral tariffs.
promises.verdict_reasoning: Corker publicly opposed Trump's Section 232 steel and aluminum tariffs and co-sponsored a resolution to require congressional approval, but it did not advance. NAFTA was renegotiated as USMCA, signed Nov 30, 2018.

politician.name: Claire Conner McCaskill
politician.current_status: Former senator (2019); defeated 2018 by Josh Hawley
politician.professional_background: U.S. Senator (MO) 2007-2019; previously Missouri State Auditor; defeated for re-election in 2018 by then-AG Josh Hawley.
politician.profile_narrative: McCaskill served two Senate terms before losing to Josh Hawley in 2018. This profile grades her 2012-cycle second term (Jan 2013-Jan 2019), notable for her work on military sexual assault, opioid investigations, and her conservative-leaning votes in a state Trump won by 18 points.

promises.promise_text: Address military sexual assault.
promises.verdict_reasoning: McCaskill's Victim Protection Act passed the Senate 97-0 on March 10, 2014 and was included in the FY2015 NDAA. She blocked Sen. Gillibrand's broader prosecutorial-discretion reform; that reform did not pass during her term.

promises.promise_text: Protect the Affordable Care Act.
promises.verdict_reasoning: McCaskill voted no on the skinny repeal (failed 49-51, July 28, 2017) and on the BCRA. She voted yes on every Democratic effort to defend ACA marketplaces.

promises.promise_text: Investigate opioid manufacturers and pharmacy benefit managers.
promises.verdict_reasoning: McCaskill as Senate Homeland Security ranking member led extensive opioid investigations releasing reports on Insys Therapeutics (Sep 2017) and Purdue Pharma; her work contributed to the bipartisan SUPPORT Act (Pub.L. 115-271, Oct 24, 2018).

promises.promise_text: Vote against unqualified Trump nominees.
promises.verdict_reasoning: McCaskill voted no on Gorsuch (April 7, 2017) and no on Kavanaugh (Oct 6, 2018). She voted yes on a number of Trump cabinet picks (Mattis, Kelly, Sessions).

promises.promise_text: Vote against the TCJA tax bill.
promises.verdict_reasoning: McCaskill voted no on the TCJA (Dec 20, 2017, 51-48). She had argued throughout that the bill disproportionately benefited corporations and high earners.

promises.promise_text: Strengthen veterans health care access.
promises.verdict_reasoning: McCaskill voted yes on the Veterans Choice Act (Pub.L. 113-146, Aug 7, 2014) and the VA MISSION Act (Pub.L. 115-182, June 6, 2018), both substantially expanding non-VA care options.

promises.promise_text: Address rising prescription drug prices.
promises.verdict_reasoning: McCaskill led a Senate investigation into pharma pricing and EpiPen pricing in particular. She introduced the Empower Act and other legislation to allow drug importation. None of her drug-pricing legislation became law.

promises.promise_text: Oppose privatization of Social Security and Medicare.
promises.verdict_reasoning: McCaskill voted no on every privatization or premium-support proposal that received a vote. No major entitlement-restructuring legislation reached final passage during her term.

promises.promise_text: Support balanced approach on immigration.
promises.verdict_reasoning: McCaskill voted yes on the Gang of Eight bill (June 27, 2013). She voted yes on Feb 2018 amendments to provide DACA-recipient protections in exchange for $25B in border-security funding; the package failed.

promises.promise_text: Protect Missouri agricultural interests from trade-war damage.
promises.verdict_reasoning: McCaskill publicly opposed Trump's tariffs and the resulting Chinese retaliatory tariffs on U.S. soybeans, but no legislation she introduced to constrain tariff authority advanced. Missouri soybean prices fell ~20% mid-2018.
